# What Are the Key Challenges in Smart Textiles?

![Contrasting textures of raw wood and dark botanical foliage create a minimalist and sustainable design study. Upcycled textiles and organic forms are highlighted, showcasing ethical fashion through raw materials. This composition embodies mindful consumption and eco-conscious style with its emphasis on natural elements and repurposed wood, contributing to a sophisticated, sustainable aesthetic in design.](https://fashion.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/botanical-elements-and-raw-wood-branches-in-dark-sustainable-fashion-design.jpg "Botanical Elements and Raw Wood Branches in Dark Sustainable Fashion Design Still Life")

## Fundamentals

Smart textiles, also known as e-textiles, represent a **fusion of traditional fabrics with electronic components**, offering a range of functionalities from health monitoring to interactive displays. However, their development and widespread adoption face considerable hurdles. These challenges are not merely technical; they extend into areas of sustainability, cost, and consumer acceptance.

![A study in sustainable style utilizing repurposed fabrics and upcycled materials set against dark granular textures and discarded plastic, this minimalist composition evokes a powerful message of conscious design. Exploring ethical fashion through visual contrast, the image promotes responsible consumption and a zero waste philosophy, showcasing beauty in eco friendly and raw elements that resonate with circularity and environmental responsibility. The draped textile and reclaimed surface highlight innovative approaches to sustainable textiles and minimalist aesthetics.](https://fashion.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/minimalist-still-life-exploring-sustainable-design-through-upcycled-textiles.jpg "Minimalist Still Life Exploring Sustainable Design through Upcycled Textiles and Dark Aesthetics")

### Technical Hurdles

One of the primary obstacles is the **integration of electronics into textiles** without compromising the fabric’s flexibility, comfort, and durability. Electronic components need to withstand the rigors of daily wear, including washing, stretching, and abrasion. Creating miniaturized, flexible, and robust electronic components that can seamlessly integrate into fabrics remains a significant technical challenge. Furthermore, ensuring the **long-term reliability** of these embedded electronics under various environmental conditions is crucial for the practicality of smart textiles.

Another technical issue is the power source. Many smart textile applications require a continuous power supply, which can be challenging to integrate into clothing without adding bulk or discomfort. **Developing efficient, lightweight, and flexible power solutions**, such as textile-based batteries or [energy Meaning → The Energy domain delves into the transition from fossil fuels to renewable sources and the future of power generation.](https://energy.sustainability-directory.com/)  harvesting technologies, is essential for the widespread use of smart textiles. Data transmission and storage also pose challenges, requiring reliable methods for collecting, processing, and transmitting data generated by the embedded sensors.

The seamless integration of electronics into textiles without compromising comfort and durability remains a significant technical hurdle.

![Black textile waste, likely post consumer material from the fashion industry, is disposed of around a concrete structure, highlighting the unsustainable linear economy and lack of circularity. The image underscores the urgent need for ethical sourcing, mindful consumption, and sustainable design practices to address the environmental impact of textile waste, promoting resource efficiency and responsible waste management and shifting away from landfill reliance and towards circularity and post consumer reuse strategies.](https://fashion.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/fashion-textile-waste-from-industry-disposed-around-concrete-structure.jpg "Fashion Textile Waste from Industry Disposed around Concrete Structure Highlights Post Consumer Material in Linear Economy and Lack of Circularity")

### Sustainability Concerns

The environmental impact of smart textiles is a growing concern. The production of electronic components often involves the use of **hazardous materials and energy-intensive processes**. The disposal of e-textiles at the end of their life cycle also presents a significant challenge, as these products cannot be easily recycled using conventional textile recycling methods. The presence of electronic components in the fabric makes it difficult to separate the materials for recycling, leading to potential environmental pollution.

The **sustainability of the materials** used in smart textiles is also a point of concern. Many smart textiles rely on synthetic materials, which are derived from fossil fuels and contribute to greenhouse gas emissions. Finding sustainable alternatives to these materials, such as bio-based polymers or recycled fibers, is crucial for reducing the environmental footprint of smart textiles. There is a pressing need for eco-friendly production methods and circular economy models for smart textiles to mitigate their environmental impact.

![Abstract layered design featuring black recycled textures and geometric forms embodies sustainable fashion and minimalist style. This composition promotes eco-design principles and waste reduction for conscious consumers seeking ethical fashion choices. Highlighting innovative sustainable materials and responsible production, the piece reflects enduring and timeless design ideals within a circular economy framework.](https://fashion.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/abstract-composition-of-layered-black-recycled-material-showcasing-sustainable.jpg "Abstract Composition of Layered Black Recycled Material Showcasing Sustainable Fashion Design and Minimalist Geometric Style")

### Cost and Scalability

The cost of smart textiles is currently a major barrier to their widespread adoption. The specialized materials and manufacturing processes required for integrating electronics into fabrics make these products significantly more expensive than traditional textiles. **Reducing the production costs** is essential to make smart textiles accessible to a broader consumer base. Scaling up production while maintaining quality and reliability is also a considerable challenge.

The **complex supply chains** involved in the production of smart textiles add to the cost and complexity. These supply chains often involve multiple stakeholders, including material suppliers, electronic component manufacturers, and textile producers. Streamlining these supply chains and developing efficient manufacturing processes are necessary to reduce costs and increase the scalability of smart textile production.

![Black recycled textiles are draped artistically across rough concrete blocks, showcasing a minimalist fashion design approach focused on sustainable practices. Dry branches interspersed throughout the scene bring in an organic contrast, highlighting the juxtaposition of natural and industrial elements. This image speaks to themes of waste reduction and eco-conscious material selection within ethical fashion.](https://fashion.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/sustainable-fashion-design-featuring-recycled-textiles-draped-on-concrete.jpg "Sustainable Fashion Design Featuring Recycled Textiles Draped on Concrete Blocks for Minimalist and Eco Conscious Aesthetics")

## Intermediate

Moving beyond the fundamental challenges, the adoption of smart textiles is further complicated by issues of **consumer acceptance and ethical considerations**. These factors are critical in determining the long-term viability and success of this innovative technology. It’s not enough for smart textiles to be technologically feasible; they must also align with consumer needs and societal values.

![Intricate textural folds of black garment fabric are presented in a dramatically lit abstract close up highlighting sustainable design principles. The image emphasizes recycled textiles and zero waste fashion aesthetics. Shadow play enhances the depth and tactile nature of the material promoting ethical and conscious design in slow fashion.](https://fashion.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/textural-abstract-black-garment-fabric-folds-representing-sustainable-and.jpg "Textural Abstract Black Garment Fabric Folds Representing Sustainable and Ethical Fashion Design Movement")

### Consumer Acceptance

One of the major hurdles is the **perception of smart textiles** among consumers. Many people are still unfamiliar with the concept of wearable technology and may be skeptical about its benefits. Overcoming this skepticism requires effective communication and education about the potential applications of smart textiles. Demonstrating the practical value of these products, such as their ability to improve health monitoring or enhance safety, is essential to gain consumer trust.

**Comfort and aesthetics** are also crucial factors in consumer acceptance. Smart textiles need to feel and look like regular clothing to be appealing to consumers. Bulky or uncomfortable electronic components can make smart textiles less desirable.

Designers and engineers must work together to create smart textiles that are both functional and fashionable. Addressing concerns about privacy and data security is also important to ensure that consumers feel comfortable wearing these products.

![Minimalist urban scene featuring a grey concrete curb bordering dark asphalt, with dry branches scattered around a reflecting puddle. The image emphasizes sustainable design through its raw aesthetic and focus on material texture and natural elements. This eco-conscious composition embodies environmental awareness, highlighting responsible urban design and a mindful approach to integrating natural forms within the built environment, promoting a sustainable and understated style with inherent textural beauty.](https://fashion.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/sustainable-minimalist-urban-design-featuring-concrete-curb-natural-branches.jpg "Sustainable Minimalist Urban Design Featuring Concrete Curb Natural Branches and Water Reflection Emphasizing Raw Texture")

### Ethical and Social Implications

The use of smart textiles raises important ethical questions. The **collection and storage of personal data** by smart textiles can pose significant privacy risks. Ensuring that data is collected and used responsibly is crucial to maintaining consumer trust.

Clear guidelines and regulations are needed to protect consumers’ privacy and prevent the misuse of personal data. Transparency about how data is collected, stored, and used is also essential.

The **potential for job displacement** in the traditional textile industry is another ethical concern. As smart textiles become more prevalent, there may be a decline in the demand for conventional textile products. This could lead to job losses in the textile manufacturing sector. Addressing these social implications requires proactive measures, such as providing training and support for workers to transition to new roles in the smart textile industry.

![From above, a minimalist and somber scene reveals dark, polluted water filled with urban debris and discarded industrial objects. Sparse white minimalist structures float along a linear pathway, creating a stark design against the black water, symbolizing industrial waste and environmental impact. The composition highlights sustainable waste management challenges and resource depletion, advocating for eco-conscious design, ethical production, and a circular economy to combat pollution and promote sustainable solutions like upcycling and slow fashion.](https://fashion.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/aerial-perspective-of-urban-waterways-reflecting-minimalist-design-amidst.jpg "Aerial Perspective of Urban Waterways Reflecting Minimalist Design Amidst Environmental Pollution and Sustainable Waste Management Challenges")

### Standardization and Regulation

The lack of **industry standards** and regulations is another significant challenge. The absence of clear guidelines for testing, safety, and performance of smart textiles can hinder their widespread adoption. Developing standardized testing methods and safety protocols is essential to ensure the quality and reliability of these products. Regulatory frameworks are needed to address issues of data privacy, consumer protection, and environmental impact.

**Interoperability** is also a concern. Smart textiles often rely on different communication protocols and data formats, which can make it difficult to integrate them with other devices and systems. Developing common standards for data exchange and communication is crucial to enable seamless integration and interoperability. Collaboration among industry stakeholders, researchers, and policymakers is essential to address these challenges.

Consumer skepticism, ethical considerations, and the lack of standardization all present significant obstacles to the widespread adoption of smart textiles.

| Challenge | Description | Impact |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Consumer Skepticism** | Lack of familiarity and trust in wearable technology. | Slows adoption and limits market growth. |
| **Ethical Concerns** | Privacy risks and job displacement. | Undermines public trust and social equity. |
| **Lack of Standards** | Absence of guidelines for safety and performance. | Hinders innovation and market development. |

![A pale egg is protectively encased within folds of recycled black plastic, embodying sustainable material exploration and minimalist design. This composition showcases upcycled waste in an artistic manner, promoting eco-conscious choices and ethical sourcing within fashion. The contrast between the raw egg and repurposed polymers highlights innovative materials and the principles of a circular economy, advocating for responsible production and a reduced footprint in sustainable practice.](https://fashion.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/pale-egg-encased-in-layers-of-recycled-black-plastic-sustainable-material.jpg "Pale Egg Encased in Layers of Recycled Black Plastic Sustainable Material Exploration")

### Supply Chain Transparency

**Supply chain transparency** is becoming increasingly important for consumers who want to make informed choices about the products they purchase. The complexity of the smart textile supply chain can make it difficult to trace the origin of materials and ensure ethical and sustainable practices. Consumers are increasingly demanding greater transparency from brands and manufacturers.

Adopting **blockchain technology** and other tracking systems can help to improve supply chain transparency. These technologies can provide consumers with detailed information about the origin of materials, manufacturing processes, and labor practices. Supply chain transparency can help to build trust and promote ethical and sustainable practices in the smart textile industry.

![This detailed close-up presents the linear design of a dark plant leaf, emphasizing sustainable and organic materials. The textures and deep green and black hues suggest a minimalist yet rich style suitable for eco-conscious fashion and design applications. The image evokes ideas of plant-based textiles, renewable resources, and ethical sourcing, highlighting responsible and sustainable fashion change.](https://fashion.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/sustainable-design-through-dark-textured-plant-leaf-details-revealing-organic.jpg "Sustainable Design through Dark Textured Plant Leaf Details Revealing Organic Style for Fashion Inspiration")

## Academic

From an academic standpoint, the challenges in smart textiles are deeply rooted in the **interdisciplinary nature** of the field. It requires a convergence of materials science, electronics engineering, textile technology, and computer science. This convergence creates a unique set of hurdles that need to be addressed through collaborative research and innovation. The complexity of this field necessitates a holistic approach that considers both the technical and societal aspects of smart textiles.

![Monochromatic black tones define deconstructed textiles and a bound form, representing minimalist design and sustainable fashion principles. Recycled materials and repurposed textiles are subtly highlighted, emphasizing ethical considerations and eco conscious style in a circular economy. This image prompts thought about textile waste reduction and material innovation within responsible fashion design, showcasing environmental awareness and a shift towards sustainable practices.](https://fashion.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/encapsulated-black-textile-waste-stream-in-minimalist-design-aesthetic.jpg "Encapsulated Black Textile Waste Stream in Minimalist Design Aesthetic Exploring Sustainable Fashion and Circularity")

### Material Science and Innovation

The development of **advanced materials** is at the core of smart textile innovation. Researchers are exploring new materials with enhanced electrical conductivity, flexibility, and durability. Nanomaterials, such as carbon nanotubes and graphene, are being investigated for their potential to create highly conductive and lightweight electronic components. However, the scalability and cost-effectiveness of these materials remain significant challenges.

**Bio-based materials** are also gaining attention as sustainable alternatives to traditional synthetic materials. Researchers are exploring the use of natural fibers and biopolymers to create eco-friendly smart textiles. However, the performance characteristics of these materials, such as their electrical conductivity and durability, need to be improved to meet the demands of smart textile applications. The development of new materials with enhanced properties is essential for advancing the field of smart textiles.

![A sustainable design speaker component crafted from eco-conscious materials is presented on a dark, reflective surface with organic elements. The minimalist aesthetic emphasizes responsible production and mindful manufacturing. This image highlights the product’s durable nature and timeless style, aligning with principles of circular economy and low impact design.](https://fashion.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/sustainable-design-speaker-component-reflecting-minimalist-aesthetic-eco.jpg "Sustainable Design Speaker Component Reflecting Minimalist Aesthetic Eco Conscious Materials Showcasing Responsible Innovation")

### Power and Energy Management

**Energy harvesting** is a promising area of research for powering smart textiles. Researchers are exploring various methods of harvesting energy from the environment, such as solar energy, mechanical energy, and thermal energy. Textile-based solar cells and triboelectric generators are being developed to capture energy from sunlight and body movements. However, the efficiency and reliability of these energy harvesting technologies need to be improved to meet the [power Meaning → Power is the rate at which energy is generated and consumed, with a focus on transitioning to clean and renewable sources.](https://energy.sustainability-directory.com/)  requirements of smart textiles.

**Battery technology** is another critical area of research. Researchers are working on developing lightweight, flexible, and rechargeable batteries that can be integrated into textiles. Solid-state batteries and textile-based batteries are being investigated for their potential to provide safe and reliable power for smart textiles. However, the energy density and cycle life of these batteries need to be improved to meet the demands of long-term use.

![This detailed macro photograph highlights sustainable bookbinding with its emphasis on recycled materials and minimalist design. The image showcases a textured, dark cover, possibly made from upcycled textiles, and pale beige recycled paper pages. Strong black threads bind the book, demonstrating durable and eco-conscious craftsmanship.](https://fashion.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/sustainable-book-binding-detail-showcasing-recycled-materials-and-eco-conscious.jpg "Sustainable Book Binding Detail Showcasing Recycled Materials and Eco Conscious Design Elements")

### Data Processing and Security

The **collection, processing, and transmission of data** generated by smart textiles pose significant challenges. Researchers are developing new algorithms and data processing techniques to analyze the data collected by embedded sensors. Machine learning and artificial intelligence are being used to extract meaningful information from sensor data. However, the computational resources required for data processing need to be optimized to minimize power [consumption Meaning → Consumption refers to the use of goods and services, with a sustainable focus on mindful, responsible, and circular patterns.](https://eco.sustainability-directory.com/)  and ensure real-time performance.

**Data security and privacy** are also major concerns. Researchers are exploring various methods of encrypting and securing data to protect user privacy. Secure data transmission protocols and authentication mechanisms are being developed to prevent unauthorized access to personal data. Ensuring the security and privacy of data collected by smart textiles is essential to build consumer trust and promote the responsible use of this technology.

Academic research is focusing on material innovation, energy management, and data security to overcome the complex challenges in smart textiles.

![Close up on a person in minimalist sustainable style with a hand on their neck. The design uses eco-textiles and reclaimed materials highlighting responsible production. This image represents a move toward ethical fashion and conscious consumption, advocating for durable materials and timeless design to reduce environmental impact.](https://fashion.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/conceptual-minimalist-sustainable-fashion-design-utilizing-reclaimed-materials.jpg "Conceptual Minimalist Sustainable Fashion Design Utilizing Reclaimed Materials and Ethical Production Practices")

### Cross-Cultural Considerations

The **cultural context** plays a significant role in the adoption of smart textiles. Different cultures have different norms and values regarding clothing and technology. Smart textiles need to be designed to be culturally sensitive and acceptable to different user groups. Understanding the specific needs and preferences of different cultural groups is essential for the successful adoption of smart textiles.

**Global supply chains** also present cross-cultural challenges. The production of smart textiles often involves multiple countries and cultural contexts. Ensuring fair labor practices and ethical sourcing of materials across different cultures is essential to promote sustainable and responsible production. Collaboration among stakeholders from different cultural backgrounds is necessary to address these challenges.

| Area | Research Focus | Challenges |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Material Science** | Developing advanced materials with enhanced properties. | Scalability, cost-effectiveness, and performance. |
| **Energy Management** | Improving energy harvesting and battery technologies. | Efficiency, reliability, and long-term performance. |
| **Data Processing** | Developing secure and efficient data processing techniques. | Computational resources, security, and privacy. |

![Close-up of textured, dry branches against a black backdrop highlighting raw natural beauty and sustainable design. Earthy tones and organic forms inspire minimalist and eco-friendly fashion concepts. The weathered texture suggests deconstructed, recycled and upcycled materials, fostering circular fashion.](https://fashion.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/dried-branches-intersecting-detail-emphasizing-natural-textures-for-sustainable.jpg "Dried Branches Intersecting Detail Emphasizing Natural Textures for Sustainable Fashion Design Inspiration")

### Long-Term Impacts and Future Directions

The long-term impacts of smart textiles are still largely unknown. However, it is clear that this technology has the potential to transform various industries, from healthcare to fashion. **Healthcare applications**, such as remote patient monitoring and personalized medicine, are particularly promising. Smart textiles can enable continuous monitoring of vital signs, such as heart rate, blood pressure, and body temperature, providing valuable data for medical professionals.

**Fashion and apparel applications** are also gaining attention. Smart textiles can be used to create interactive clothing with integrated sensors, displays, and communication capabilities. These applications have the potential to revolutionize the way people interact with their clothing. However, the ethical and social implications of these applications need to be carefully considered to ensure that this technology is used responsibly and ethically.

Techniques, applications, and challenges in textiles for a sustainable future
=============================================================================

Author links open overlay panel Md Tanvir Hossain a, Md Abdus Shahid b, Md Golam Mortuza Limon b, [Imam Hossain b](https://www.sciencedirect.com/author/7003992193/imam-hossain), Nadim Mahmud b

Abstract
--------

Next generation textiles (NGTs) represent a paradigm shift in the textile industry, incorporating the most recent advances in materials, technologies, and functionalities. The dynamic world of textiles is undergoing an extraordinary transition, ushering in the age of NGTs. This article discusses the mechanisms, difficulties, present advancements, and potential future opportunities associated with these NGTs. This assessment traverses the domains of nanotechnology, [3D printing](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/three-dimensional-printing "Learn more about 3D printing from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), recycling, [wearable electronics](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/wearable-electronics "Learn more about wearable electronics from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), machine learning, [biomimicry](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/biomimetics "Learn more about biomimicry from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and [energy harvesting](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/energy-harvesting "Learn more about energy harvesting from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") while focusing on sustainability, functionality, smart integration, [advanced manufacturing](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/advanced-manufacturing "Learn more about advanced manufacturing from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") techniques, and [multifunctionality](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/multifunctionality "Learn more about multifunctionality from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") as major factors. The paper highlights the wonderful prospects of improved performance, sustainability, [intelligent textiles](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/intelligent-textile "Learn more about intelligent textiles from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and [wearable technologies](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/wearable-technology "Learn more about wearable technologies from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), highlighting consumerization, personalization, safety, and protection enhancements. Furthermore, a key method for overcoming major challenges is discussed which is to adopt open innovation, which encourages collaboration, knowledge exchange, and the integration of external resources, all of which aid in addressing scalability and mass production, costing, and a lack of modern technologies in the NGTs industry.

1. Introduction
---------------

Textiles are positioned to play a significant part in determining the future of fashion, sustainability, and functionality as technology develops and consumer needs change ([Fouda et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000246#bib33), [Farooq and Zhang, 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000246#bib31)). It needs to be more functional, comfortable, responsive as well as sustainable ([Persson et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000246#bib81)). The initial purpose of textiles was to protect people from the rain and cold. Later in history, clothes began to incorporate aesthetic considerations as well ([Mostafizur Rahman et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000246#bib69)). In recent years, smart and interactive textiles have been introduced as a new generation of [textile fabrics](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/textile-fabric "Learn more about textile fabrics from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") ([Syduzzaman et al., 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000246#bib108)). Next generation textiles (NGTs) represent a paradigm shift in the textile industry, incorporating the most recent advances in materials, technologies, and functionalities. These textiles strive to meet modern demands for sustainability, advanced performance, smart capabilities, and versatility, putting them at the forefront of innovation in the [textile and apparel industries](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/textile-and-apparel-industry "Learn more about textile and apparel industries from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). These textiles go beyond traditional fabrics, incorporating aspects like [smart sensors](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/smart-sensors "Learn more about smart sensors from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), functionality, responsive materials, sustainable components, and functional coatings ([Castano and Flatau, 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000246#bib12)). Moreover, the EU took a wonderful initiative to form a strategy that aims to make all textiles sustainable ([Commission, 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000246#bib21)).

In recent years, NGTs have become more and more dependent on nanotechnology. Nanotechnology has been used to create fabrics and garments with sustainable antibacterial, UV-resistant, electrically conductive, optical, hydrophobic, and flame-retardant properties ([Hossain et al., 2023a](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000246#bib42), [Hossain, 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000246#bib41)). A wide [range of functions](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/range-of-function "Learn more about range of functions from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), including [energy harvesting](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/energy-harvesting "Learn more about energy harvesting from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and storage, sensing, drug release, and optics, are now being performed by smart textile-based nanomaterial-based systems. In addition to finding considerable use in the fashion industry, these technologies are being expanded for wider utilization in military, healthcare, and on-body energy harnessing applications ([Shah et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000246#bib98)). Another technique is [3D printing](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/three-dimensional-printing "Learn more about 3D printing from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), which can play a wide role in the case of next gen textiles. [3D printing](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/3d-printing "Learn more about 3D printing from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") can be used as stretchable elastic fibers ([Chen et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000246#bib15)), and [thermoplastic](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/thermoplastics "Learn more about thermoplastic from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")[monofilament](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/monofilament "Learn more about monofilament from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") for [smart textile](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/smart-textile "Learn more about smart textile from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), which is flexible and conductive ([Eutionnat-Diffo et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000246#bib30)). An article showed about bio-fabricated textile fabrics that were derived from algae, fungi, and other microorganisms. This [bioengineering](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/bioengineering "Learn more about bioengineering from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") technique takes the textile fabrics to the next level ([Rognoli et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000246#bib86)). Energy-harvesting technology can be incorporated into textiles to power [wearable electronics](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/wearable-electronics "Learn more about wearable electronics from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and [Internet of Things](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/internet-of-things "Learn more about Internet of Things from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (IoT) gadgets. Such next-generation fabrics can capture and store energy by energy harvesting technology ([Satharasinghe et al., 2020a](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000246#bib95), [Somkuwar et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000246#bib104)). Similarly, many technologies have been introduced for NGT, but sustainability is the prime concern ([Nishant et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000246#bib73)).

Additionally, it is crucial to ensure that [future textiles](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/future-textile "Learn more about future textiles from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") are environmentally friendly. Despite many attempts to be environmentally friendly, some techniques of producing these fabrics can still lead to significant pollution. Nature can be harmed by the way we employ microscopic materials, fabric chemicals, and waste we produce when making these textiles. Another significant area for improvement with employing NGT is safety. Due to its versatility, the product may also introduce electrical dangers, chemical exposure, or heat generation. A difficult issue for advanced textiles is user privacy and security ([Kongahage and Foroughi, 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000246#bib51), [Cherenack and Van Pieterson, 2012](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000246#bib16)).

Several reviews were done regarding the advanced and smart textiles. Cherenack et al. looked at the development, difficulties, design, embedded sensors, device connectivity, and illustrative technologies but should have mentioned the key aspects of it ([Cherenack and Van Pieterson, 2012](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000246#bib16)). Meanwhile Farooq et al. examined recent breakthroughs in wearable electronics, focusing on [data transfer](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/data-transfer "Learn more about data transfer from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), assistive robots, prosthetics, health monitoring, various wearables, and crucial insights regarding the development of electronic technology in the future without mentioning challenges ([Farooq and Zhang, 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000246#bib32)). In their article, Ramlow and his colleagues addressed the obstacles to widespread commercial production and discussed the developments in [electronic textile](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/electronic-textile "Learn more about electronic textile from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") technologies, energy sources such as energy-harvesting textiles, and [wireless power transfer](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/wireless-power-transfer "Learn more about wireless power transfer from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") ([Ramlow et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000246#bib84)). Despite the novelty of the technology, Yang et al. discovered that individuals were eager to embrace and employ smart materials and technologies in assistive technology as long as they were comfortable, aesthetically pleasing, and easy to use ([Yang and Moody, 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000246#bib123)). In addition, Oliveira and her research team also presented an overview of [intelligent textiles](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/intelligent-textile "Learn more about intelligent textiles from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), covering fundamental ideas, classifications, a variety of applications, and an emphasis on important production materials, but not mentioning the cost related to it ([de Oliveira et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000246#bib74)). Additionally, Syduzzaman and his colleagues provided a thorough analysis of the advancement of wearable electronics, covering sports, business, healthcare, and more, but they should have mentioned the procedures or techniques used ([Syduzzaman et al., 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000246#bib108)).

However, these review papers have not mentioned all the aspects of producing an NGT. This review paper discusses sustainability, functionalization, and other major aspects that are related to NGTs. Also, the obstacle associated with traditional textiles and the chronological development of the textile has illustrated that other review papers did not mention. Considering the research gap, this article demonstrates techniques like Nanotechnology, 3D printing, Recycling, Bioengineering, and so on that are implementable for next-generation textile formation with their challenges and recommendations to make it sustainable. Furthermore, there are a lot of challenges to transforming traditional textiles into advanced textiles, which are not comprehensively referred to by existing review articles. Despite that, probable challenges associated with the transformation of advanced textiles from traditional textiles have been mentioned here. The researchers need to be concerned about the cost, scalability, safety concerns, consumer behavior towards the product, and environmental impact before going through the path. The author has discussed future prospects regarding NGT, such as achieving more sustainability, [customization](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/customisation "Learn more about customization from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and safety features and functionality, in this review paper. This article aims to present a thorough analysis of current textile breakthroughs and trends to pave the way for future technological advancements of NGT by maintaining sustainability toward a greener world.

2. Introduction to textiles
---------------------------

The history of textile development is lengthy and extensive, going back thousands of years. Here is a chronological evolution of textiles unfolding as a magnificent tapestry of innovation and cultural progress across millennia as illustrated in the [Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000246#fig0005)**.**

![Image 12: Fig. 1](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2199853124000246-gr1.jpg)
1.   [Download: Download high-res image (488KB)](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2199853124000246-gr1_lrg.jpg "Download high-res image (488KB)")
2.   [Download: Download full-size image](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2199853124000246-gr1.jpg "Download full-size image")

Fig. 1. Chronological development of the textiles over the centuries.

From the basic clothing of the prehistoric era to the cutting-edge materials of the 21st century, textiles have continually evolved to human requirements and technical breakthroughs. In ancient civilizations, textiles like linen, cotton, and silk took center stage, establishing cultural identities and global trading routes. The Middle Ages brought in the supremacy of wool, while the Renaissance introduced mechanized looms, promoting industrialization. The 19th century brought the development of synthetic colors and the revolution of [sewing machines](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/sewing-machines "Learn more about sewing machines from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). The 20th century witnessed a synthetic fiber revolution, the emergence of technical textiles, and the rise of digital printing. The 21st century's focus switched to [smart textiles](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/smart-textile "Learn more about smart textiles from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), coupling materials with electronics, nanotechnology, and sustainability, pushing the bounds of innovation. This chronological perspective underlines how textiles, woven into the fabric of human history, have continuously adapted to changing times, needs, and opportunities. The historical progression of textiles demonstrates how human creativity and technological progress have continuously altered the manufacture and functionality of fabrics. Textile advances in sustainable practices, innovative materials, and intelligent technology continue to shape the future of the textile business. Additionally, Conventional textiles have a number of urgent concerns, including their considerable environmental effect, production of wasteful practices, dependency on scarce resources, limited usefulness, lack of sustainability, and issues relating to chemical consumption, worker conditions, and consumer awareness. These obstacles demonstrate the need for a more innovative and ecological approach to [textile manufacture](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/textile-processing "Learn more about textile manufacture from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), usage, and disposal. By embracing technological advancements, implementing sustainable practices, and promoting awareness, the textile industry can lessen these problems and create a new opportunity for more responsible and forward-thinking textiles.

3. Key aspect of NGTs
---------------------

Key aspects of NGT have discussed in this section and [Fig. 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000246#fig0010) illustrated it shortly.

![Image 13: Fig. 2](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2199853124000246-gr2.jpg)

Fig. 2. Schematic diagram on the key aspect of NGTs.

### 3.1. Sustainability

NGT places a high priority on sustainability, using sustainable materials, renewable energy ideas, and responsible manufacturing techniques to reduce their overall environmental effect. In particular, El-Naggar and his coworkers prepared a special cotton swab that can test sweat pH for healthcare and drug testing. They used a substance from a type of cabbage to dye the cotton and make it change color based on pH. The dyeing process didn't affect the fabric's quality and also a sustainable product ([El-Naggar et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000246#bib29)). Again, Yin et al. reviewed that advanced textiles may be successfully incorporated during regular wears. It is useful to convert on-body biomechanical, [biochemical](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/biochemicals "Learn more about biochemical from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and body [heat energy](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/thermal-energy "Learn more about heat energy from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") into electrical signals for long-term, real-time monitoring of [physiological conditions](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/physiological-condition "Learn more about physiological conditions from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). This can lessen energy consumption and enhance sustainability ([Yin et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000246#bib125)). Furthermore, Veeramuthu and colleagues created [wearable electronics](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/wearable-electronics "Learn more about wearable electronics from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") that are strong, flexible, and pressure-tolerant, inspired by muscle fibers, suited for [energy harvesting](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/energy-harvesting "Learn more about energy harvesting from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), light-up gadgets, and smart gloves, using environmentally friendly manufacturing techniques ([Veeramuthu et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000246#bib112)). Meanwhile, Attia et al. reviewed recent developments in the creation of UV-protective coatings for sustainable textiles ([Attia et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000246#bib4)).

### 3.2. Functionalization

By using advanced technology, these textiles are designed for certain applications, improving characteristics like water resistance, antibacterial effects, flame resistance, and temperature regulation using additives, coatings, or nanomaterial’s as drawn in [Figure 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000246#fig0010). For example, Quartinello and his research team developed an eco-friendly wound dressing employing cotton/PET blends filled with albumin/silk fibroin [nanocapsules](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/nanocapsule "Learn more about nanocapsules from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") containing natural antimicrobial agents. As it is determined to inhabit the raising of antibiotic resistance in bacteria that has a vital role in environmental safety ([Quartinello et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000246#bib82)). Moreover, Zhao et al. used an intensive responsible, and long lasting smart [textile sensor](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/textile-sensor "Learn more about textile sensor from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") that were produced from flexible nylon thread with [carbon nanotubes](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/c

URL: https://media.licdn.com/dms/image/D5612AQGRR-lXV5W1bw/article-cover_image-shrink_720_1280/0/1669183459070?e=2147483647&v=beta&t=uYa28G0ekdiEVdjACbFeo1uqtLh8JkRjEjsqUTh-4kM
DESCRIPTION: The image juxtaposes customer expectations for smart textile products, emphasizing low cost, comfort, and reliability, with product development challenges such as high costs, complex processes, and achieving garment reliability.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

enriched_points.expanded_content: The high cost of specialized materials and complex manufacturing processes currently limits the affordability of smart textiles, hindering their widespread market acceptance and scalability.
Lack of consumer familiarity with smart textiles contributes to skepticism. Effective education and communication about their benefits are essential to encourage acceptance and adoption.
Seamless integration of smart textiles into current clothing systems poses technical challenges. Compatibility with existing technologies and infrastructure is crucial for successful implementation.
